entries.ocr_text:
FOREWORD
The 1954 General Assembly , through the enactment of
Senate Bill No. 140, imposed upon the Legislative Research
Commission the duty of furnishing the printer correct copy of
bills and resolutions for inclusion in the Session Acts . Section
57.180 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes stipulates that bound
copies of Session Acts be delivered and distributed within sixty
days after the end of a regular session .
This date limit is based on sound reasons . The majority
of legislation enacted by any session of the General Assembly
becomes effective as law ninety days after adjournment . Re-
sponsible public officers and other interested citizens need to
have a copy of the Session Acts prior to the date the laws become
effective . Records do not indicate that this date has ever been
met . The most prompt delivery in recent years has been some
120 days following the close of the session .
In the preparation of this volume of the 1956 Session Acts
the Secretary of State has furnished access to copy of enrolled
bills and resolutions as filed in her office . The staff of the Legis-
lative Research Commission read the prepared page proofs with
the enrolled bills . In order to expedite furnishing copy to the
printer , bills and resolutions are inserted by chapters according
to the order in which they received gubernatorial action and were
filed in the office of the Secretary of State. James T. Fleming
on the staff of the Commission has prepared , edited and indexed
this volume of the Session Acts .
Frankfort , Kentucky
April 18, 1956
HARRY LEE WATERFIELD
Chairman
Legislative Research Commission

classification.summary: This act details the procedures for the chief clerk of each house to furnish daily journals to the Legislative Research Commission. It outlines the commission's role in comparing and correcting proofs of the journals with the public printer. The act also establishes a Legislative Session Fund for compensation and expenses of the General Assembly, to be paid on vouchers approved by the chairman of the Legislative Research Commission. Finally, it addresses the payment of expenses for preparing, indexing, and editing journals and amends Section 6.160 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes regarding the filing and preservation of journals.

It focuses on the handling of official journals, the management of legislative funds, and the amendment of an existing statute concerning journal preservation. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
4 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 3
(Н. В. 40)
AN ACT relating to the Workmen's Compensation Board .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 342.215 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows :
( 1 ) The Workmen's Compensation Board shall consist of
five members appointed by the Governor and shall be a part of
the Department of Industrial Relations .
( 2 ) Each member of the board shall hold office for four
years and until his successor is appointed and qualified . Upon
the expiration of the term of any member , his successor shall be
appointed for a full term of four years . Vacancies shall be filled
by appointment for the unexpired term .
( 3 ) The senior member in length of service on the board
in his current term in any given year shall be chairman during
that year . No appointee to fill an unexpired term shall succeed
his predecessor in the chairmanship of the board. The member
who would , but for the vacancy , have been chairman shall at
once succeed to the unexpired chairmanship , when , a vacancy
occurs therein , in addition to the year's chairmanship to which
he would otherwise have been entitled .
Whereas , the volume of work now before the Workmen's
Compensation Board is so great that the board as presently con-
stituted must of necessity delay making decisions on many im-
portant matters pending before it ; and ,
Whereas , it is vitally important to the welfare of the
people of the Commonwealth of Kentucky that all matters pend-
ing before the board be determined as expediently as possible ,
Now , Therefore , an emergency is hereby declared to exist,
and this Act shall become effective upon
itspassage and approval
by the Governor
.
Approved January 25
, 1956
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1956, p. 25

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the Workmen's Compensation Board.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 342.215 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to increase the number of members on the Workmen's Compensation Board from an unspecified number to five. It also clarifies the appointment process, term lengths, and the selection of the chairman. The stated purpose is to address the backlog of cases before the board.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the structure and operation of the Workmen's Compensation Board. It details the number of board members, their appointment, terms, and how the chairman is selected. The stated justification for the act is the volume of work before the board, implying a need for increased capacity. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACT' from the 'GENERAL ASSEMBLY' in '1956'. The category is 'labor' because it pertains to workmen's compensation.

CHAPTER 4 5
CHAPTER 4
(Н. В. 42)
AN ACT relating to military affairs .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 36.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows :
The Governor , immediately on his induction into office,
shall appoint the Adjutant General , who shall be not less than
thirty years of age and who shall have served honorably , or
be serving , as a commissioned officer of the armed forces of
the United States . The Governor shall issue a commission to
the Adjutant General in the grade commensurate with the
duties and responsibilities of the office. If the commission is
only temporary , it shall automatically be vacated when the
Adjutant General is relieved from duty or at the expiration
of the term of the Governor appointing him . The Adjutant
General shall execute bond in the sum of ten thousand dollars .
Section 2. Section 36.030 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
The office of the Adjutant General , Deputy Adjutant
General , or Assistant Adjutant General shall not be incompatible
with or serve to vacate the commission in the Kentucky National
Guard held by the officer appointed as the Adjutant General ,
Deputy Adjutant General or Assistant Adjutant General at
the time of his appointment , nor shall it be incompatible with
any office to which he might be promoted or appointed , nor
interfere with or prevent his attending to the duties of the
office held at the time or to which he may be thereafter
promoted or appointed .
Section 3. The Governor may appoint a Deputy Adjutant
General for the Army National Guard and a Deputy Adjutant
General for the Air National Guard, who , at the time of ap-
pointment shall be at least thirty years of age and shall have
served honorably , or be serving , as a commissioned officer of
the armed forces of the United States . The Governor shall
issue a commission to such Deputy Adjutant General in the
grade commensurate with the duties and responsibilities of that

classification.title: AN ACT relating to pari mutuel and other similar systems of betting, including the taxation thereof.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes Section 138.510 to adjust the excise tax rate on pari mutuel betting at horse race tracks. It imposes a 4% tax on the first eighteen million dollars wagered and 6% on amounts exceeding eighteen million dollars. A new section is added to exempt associations or corporations that do not pay dividends or distribute profits to stockholders or members, provided all profits are used solely for scientific, educational, or charitable purposes by other qualified organizations within the United States, with no benefit inuring to any stockholder or member.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns taxation of pari mutuel betting at horse race tracks and provides an exemption for certain non-profit entities whose profits are dedicated to scientific, educational, or charitable purposes. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act amends KRS 28.440 to allow for the adjustment of salaries for criminal court reporters in counties with a population of 150,000 or more. The salaries are fixed by law but can be increased or decreased upon written recommendation from presiding judges to the fiscal court, which then requires approval by resolution of the fiscal court. Reporters are to receive no other compensation from the county or state for their services in these divisions, except as otherwise provided.

It details how fees are paid by parties, including provisions for indigent parties (suing in forma pauperis) where the county may cover costs. It also specifies salary arrangements for reporters in different court branches (common pleas and chancery) in counties with a population of 150,000 or more, allowing for adjustments to these salaries based on recommendations from the general term of the circuit court and approval by the fiscal court. Provisions for duplicate transcripts are also included.

classification.title: Policemen's and Firemen's Retirement Fund of the City of____ . .___ .
classification.summary: This act establishes a retirement fund for members of the police and fire departments in cities of the second class in Kentucky. The fund is intended to provide retirement annuities, disability benefits, and benefits for dependents, aiming to encourage qualified personnel to enter and remain in service.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes the establishment of a retirement fund for police and fire department members in cities of the second class in Kentucky. The text focuses on the purpose and structure of the fund, including provisions for retirement annuities, disability benefits, and benefits for dependents. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act relating to retirement annuities for city employees
classification.summary: This act details the eligibility and computation of service credit for retirement annuities for city employees. It outlines various types of service that qualify for credit, including service in city departments, civilian service for city, state, or federal government, and military service during national emergencies. It also specifies how partial years of service are calculated and sets conditions for early retirement and deferred annuities.

Section 9. The rate of retirement annuity shall be 2½ per-
cent of average salary, as defined in section ( 3 ) ( m ) of this
Act , for each year of total service up to and including 20 years ,
one percent of average salary for each of the next 5 years of
total service , one-half percent of average salary for each year
of total service after 25 years , subject to a maximum of 60 per-
cent of average salary . Fractional periods of service shall be
considered in the calculation of such annuities according to the
aforesaid rate .
Section 10. ( 1 ) Upon death of a member due to occu-
pational causes , regardless of length of service , his surviving
widow shall be entitled immediately upon cessation of salary to
an annuity equal to 50 percent of the member's last rate of salary .
This annuity shall be payable until she dies or remarries . In
addition , if minor children , under age 18, survive the member ,
the widow shall receive on account of each child, 10 percent of
the member's last rate of salary until each child attains age 18.
The combined payments to a widow and minor children shall
not exceed 75 percent of his final rate of salary .
(2 ) If a widow does not survive the member , or if she
remarries , and minor children under age 18 exist, each child shall
be entitled to an annuity equal to 15 percent of the member's last
rate of salary until each child attains age 18, provided that the
combined payments to such children shall not exceed 50 percent
of the member's last rate of salary .
( 3 ) If neither a widow nor minor children , eligible for
benefits survive the member , each dependent parent shall be
entitled to an annuity equal to 25 percent of the member's last
rate of salary , or 50 percent to both parents .

classification.title: ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
classification.summary: This section of the Acts of the General Assembly from 1956 details provisions related to retirement benefits, death benefits, and refunds of contributions for members of a fund. It outlines how death benefits are calculated, the minimum payment, and how survivor annuities affect the death benefit determination. It also covers refunds for members withdrawing from service before retirement, the forfeiture of rights upon refund, and the process for re-employment and restoration of credited service. Finally, it mandates that active members contribute six percent of their current salary, with deductions made by the city's director of finance.

classification.title: An Act relating to the establishment and operation of a retirement fund for city employees.
classification.summary: This act outlines the duties of a board responsible for establishing and maintaining a retirement fund for city employees. These duties include managing the fund's office, holding meetings, keeping records, establishing accounting systems, adopting actuarial tables, obtaining necessary information, processing applications for annuities and benefits, accepting gifts and bequests, and having the fund's accounts audited annually. The board is also authorized to promulgate rules and regulations for the proper determination of claims.

CHAPTER 26
(Н. В. 97)
AN ACT relating to unlawful cutting of timber .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows :
( 1 ) Any person who unlawfully enters upon and cuts or
saws down , or causes to be cut or sawed down , timber growing
upon the land of another and without color of title in himself to
the timber , or to the land upon which the timber was growing ,
shall be liable to the rightful owner of the timber in punitive
damages .
( 2) This Act shall not be construed as repealing any of
the provisions of section 433.260 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes and any penalties provided by this Act shall be considered
as additional thereto .
Approved February 8, 1956

(Н. В. 106 )
AN ACT relating to personnel appointed by the Governor , amending
sections 11.040 and 11.110 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes and
repealing section 11.030 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 11.030 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is repealed .

classification.title: AN ACT relating to unlawful cutting of timber.
classification.summary: This act creates a new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the unlawful cutting of timber. It establishes that any person who unlawfully enters land and cuts timber without color of title is liable for punitive damages to the rightful owner. It also clarifies that this act does not repeal KRS 433.260 and that penalties under this act are additional.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains three distinct acts from the 1956 General Assembly. The first act (Section 10 and 11) refers to other KRS sections and repeals others, but does not contain substantive law. The second act (Chapter 26) is an 'AN ACT relating to unlawful cutting of timber.' This act defines penalties for unlawfully cutting timber on another's land. It does not mention race or have any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The third act (Chapter 27) is an 'AN ACT relating to personnel appointed by the Governor,' amending and repealing sections of the Kentucky Revised Statutes related to gubernatorial appointments. This act also has no racial component. Therefore, none of these acts are Jim Crow laws.

such duties as the Governor may require of them . The per-
sons so appointed shall hold office at the pleasure of the Gov-
ernor . None of the provisions of KRS 64.640 shall be applicable
to persons appointed under this section .
Section 3. Section 11.110 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
The Governor shall fix the compensation of persons em-
ployed by him under the provisions of KRS 11.040 and 11.070 .
If the services of the person employed are rendered in or to
any particular agency , his compensation and expenses may be
charged to and paid out of the appropriation of that agency ,
when certified to the Department of Finance by the head of the
agency and approved by the Governor . If there is no appro-
priation to the agency from which such compensation and ex-
penses can properly be paid , the Governor may direct that they
be paid out of the Governor's Emergency Fund.
Approved February 8, 1956

AN ACT relating to city police and fire departments .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 95.550 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) The pensions or benefits paid for disability or death
from the policemen's and firemen's pension fund in cities of the
third class shall be as follows :
( a) If any member of the police or fire department be-
comes temporarily totally disabled , physically or mentally ,
while in the performance of duty and by reason of service in
the department , the board of trustees of the pension fund shall
order paid to him monthly , during his disability but not longer
than one year , a sum of not more than sixty dollars per month ,
the amount to be determined by the board . This provision shall
not apply if a salary is paid during the same period . Provided ,
however , that the provisions of this paragraph shall not apply
unless the disabled fireman or policeman has served from one
day to ten years consecutively in his department , such period of
service to be fixed by the board of trustees .
(b ) If any member of the police or fire department be-
comes permanently disabled , physically or mentally , while in
the performance of duty and by reason of service in the depart-
ment , so as to render necessary his retirement from service in
the department , the board of trustees shall retire him from
service and order paid to him monthly fifty percent of his
monthly salary at the time of his retirement. Provided , how-
ever , that the provisions of this paragraph shall not apply unless
the disabled fireman or policeman has served from one day to ten
years consecutively in his department , such period of service
to be fixed by the board .

( c) If any member of the police or fire department is
killed or dies as the result of an injury received in the perform-
ance of duty , or dies of any disease contracted by reason of his
occupation , or dies while in the service from any cause as a
result of his service in the department , or dies in service or
while on the retired list from any cause after fifteen consecutive
years of service in the department , and leaves a widow or a
child under fourteen years of age the board of trustees shall
order a pension paid to the widow or child . There shall be
paid monthly to the widow, while unmarried , a pension of
thirty dollars , and for each child until it reaches the age of
fourteen years , six dollars . If the deceased member was un-
married and childless , a pension shall be paid to his dependent
father and mother of thirty dollars per month . If one parent
is dead , the other shall receive the entire thirty dollars , and if
both are living , each shall receive fifteen dollars .
( 2) No person shall receive a pension from the policemen's
and firemen's pension fund except as provided in this section .
Section 2. Section 95.580 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) The policemen's and firemen's pension fund in cities
of the third class shall consist of :
( a) The proceeds of an annual tax, which may be levied
by the city legislative body , of not less than one cent nor more
than four cents on each one hundred dollars in value of taxable
property.
(b ) All rewards , fees, gifts and emoluments paid or given
on account of extraordinary service of any member of the police
or fire department .
( c ) Assessments , which the board of trustees of the pen-
sion fund may make , upon each member of the police and fire
departments , of not less than one percent nor more than four
percent of his salary, to be withheld from the monthly salary
and paid by the city treasurer into the pension fund.
( 2) Both the principal and interest of the pension fund
shall be applicable to the payment of pensions in cities of the
third class .
Approved February 8, 1956

CHAPTER 34

AN ACT relating to taxation in cities of fifth and sixth classes .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 92.270 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows :
( 1) The city council of each city of the fifth class , and the
board of trustees of any city of the sixth class that does not
adopt the county assessment as made , shall appoint three discreet
property owners as a board of equalization .
( 2 ) Each member of the board in a city of the fifth class
shall receive five dollars for each day of actual service , and in a
city of the sixth class not more than five dollars for each day of
actual service .
(3) The city clerk shall act as clerk of the board of
equalization .
Section 2. Section 92.420 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows :
Property subject to city taxation shall be assessed for city
taxation as of the following dates :
( 1) In cities of the second class , July 1 .
( 2) In cities of the third class , a date fixed by the city
legislative body .
( 3 ) In cities of the fourth class , April 1 .
(4) In cities of the fifth class , January 1 .
(5) In cities of the sixth class, January 1 .

shall be expended by each Commonwealth's attorney in his dis-
cretion , the manner in which the same shall be applied in pay-
ment or reimbursement of such expenses being determined by
such officer.

the provisions of this Act, a tax of two dollars is hereby levied
upon conviction in each criminal case carrying a fine where the

deposited into a revolving fund which hereby is created and

and from which fund the appropriations hereunder shall be dis-
bursed.

classification.title: AN ACT concerning the levy of license taxes in cities of the first class
classification.summary: This act amends Section 91.200 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, allowing boards of aldermen in first-class cities to impose license fees on franchises, businesses, trades, occupations, professions, and the use of animals, articles, or other things for revenue purposes. It specifies that these license fees can be imposed at a percentage rate not exceeding certain limits on salaries, wages, commissions, and other compensation earned within the city for work performed there. The act also addresses the disposition of these taxes among sinking funds, general funds, and special capital funds, and restricts ad valorem taxes for sinking fund purposes. An emergency is declared due to economic conditions impacting Commonwealth's attorneys.

CHAPTER 41
(Н. В. 147)
AN ACT relating to school busses .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
No school bus shall be licensed or operated for the trans-
portation of school children unless it has a folding sign on the
driver's side thereof , with letters at least six inches in height and
containing the words "Stop. " Upon stopping the school bus the
driver shall open out the sign so that it will be plainly visible
to traffic approaching from both directions .
For failure to display the sign when the bus is stopped while
loading or unloading school children , the driver shall be fined not
less than ten nor more than one hundred dollars .

defamatory statement by a visual or sound radio broadcast , the
plaintiff shall recover no more than special damages unless he
shall allege and prove that he made sufficient demand for correc-
tion and that the radio or television broadcasting station, failed
to make conspicuous and timely publication of said correction .
(b ) A "sufficient demand for correction " is a demand for
correction which is in writing; which is signed by the plaintiff
or his duly authorized attorney or agent ; which specifies the
statement or statements claimed to be false and defamatory ,
states wherein they are false , and sets forth the true facts ; and
which is delivered to the defendant prior to the commencement
of the action .
( c ) A “ correction " is either ( i) the publication of an ac-
knowledgment that the statement or statements specified as false
and defamatory in the plaintiff's demand for correction are er-
roneous , or ( ii ) the publication of the plaintiff's statement of
the true facts ( as set forth in his demand for correction ) or a
fair summary thereof , exclusive of any portions thereof which
are defamatory of another , obscene, or otherwise improper for
publication . If the demand for correction has specified two or
more statements as false and defamatory , the correction may deal
with some of such statements pursuant to ( i) above and with
other of such statements pursuant to ( ii ) above .
(d) A "conspicuous publication " in a visual or sound radio
broadcast is a publication which is broadcast at substantially the
same time of day, and with the same sending power , as the
statement or statements specified as false and defamatory in the
demand for correction . A publication in a particular manner
which is agreeable to the plantiff shall in any event be deemed
"conspicuous ."
( e ) A "timely publication " in a visual or sound radio
broadcast is a publication within one business day after the day
on which a sufficient demand for correction is received by the
defendant . A "business day " is any day other than a Sunday or
legal holiday . A publication on a particular day which is agree-
able to the plaintiff shall in any event be deemed "timely . "
( f ) "Special damages " are pecuniary damages which the
plaintiff alleges and proves that he has suffered in respect to his
property , business , trade , profession , or occupation ( including
such amounts of money as the plaintiff alleges and proves he has

Section 67.110 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows :
( 1 ) Each county commissioner serving on a fiscal court
may be paid out of the county treasury the following compensa-
tion for his services :
( a) In counties containing a city of the first class , a salary
of five thousand dollars per annum , payable monthly ;
(b ) In counties containing a city of the second class , a
salary of four thousand dollars , payable monthly ;
( c) In counties not containing a city of the second class
but having a population of 75,000 or over , a salary of three thou-
sand six hundred dollars , payable monthly ;
(d) In counties containing a city of the third or fourth
class and population is under 75,000 , a salary of not more than
two thousand eight hundred dollars per annum , payable monthly ;
( e ) In other counties , the same compensation per day as
justices of the peace receive under KRS 64.530 .
Approved February 16 , 1956

classification.title: An Act relating to tobacco weighing operations
classification.summary: This act mandates that all equipment used for transporting tobacco baskets during weighing operations in tobacco warehouses, redryers, prize rooms, or companies must be of uniform weight. It also specifies that if the equipment is not owned by the establishment, the weighman must use the tare weight set by the Division of Weights and Measures. The basket tare must also remain consistent with the original determination by the Division of Weights and Measures. Violations of section 1 are punishable by fines of $200-$500 per offense, and violations of section 2 (failure to post the law) are punishable by fines of $50-$100 per day.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the standardization of weights and measures for tobacco handling equipment in commercial settings. It outlines regulations for weighing operations and penalties for non-compliance. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is purely on commercial regulation and weights and measures accuracy. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 317.100 concerning barbering and beauty culture licensing
classification.summary: This statute amends KRS 317.100 to outline the requirements for individuals seeking barbering or beauty culture licenses in Kentucky, particularly those who have practiced in other states or countries. It specifies age, moral character, and educational/licensing prerequisites for both professions. For barbers, it allows acceptance for examination if they meet age, character, and substantially equivalent licensing requirements from another jurisdiction, or if they can prove educational requirements and three years of practice in a state without a barber or beauty culture law. For beauty specialists, it grants licenses upon application and payment of fees if they are Kentucky residents, at least eighteen years old, of good moral character, and licensed from a state with substantially equivalent requirements.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with professional licensing for barbers and beauty specialists. It outlines the qualifications and requirements for obtaining a license in Kentucky, including provisions for individuals licensed in other states or countries. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The requirements are based on age, moral character, education, and prior licensing, which are neutral criteria. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 58
(Н. В. 161 )
AN ACT relating to par value of insurance company stocks .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Kentucky Revised Statute 304.121 is amended to read as
follows :
( 1 ) Five or more natural persons over twenty -one years
of age may incorporate a stock insurer .
( 2 ) In addition to the applicable requirements of laws
governing the incorporation of business corporations generally :
( a ) The articles of incorporation shall specify the kind or
kinds of insurance proposed to be transacted .
(b ) The name of the corporation shall contain the words
“insurance company ".
( c ) Each share of capital stock shall have a par value of
not less than one dollar .
(d) The incorporators shall deposit the articles of incor-
poration , in quadruplicate originals , with the commissioner ,
and such articles shall not be filed with the Secretary of State
until approved by the commissioner as provided in KRS 304.123
and the commissioner's approval has been stamped upon or
otherwise attached to the articles .
Approved February 16, 1956

CHAPTER 59 83
CHAPTER 59
(Н. В. 201)
AN ACT relating to judicial districts ; creating the Forty -fifth Judicial
District , fixing the time of holding courts thereof ; changing the
Sixth , and Thirty - eighth Judicial Districts as is necessary on account
of the creation of the Forty - fifth Judicial District , and in order to
comply with the meaning and intent of the Constitution with respect
to the division of the state into judicial districts ; providing for the
continuance in office of the present circuit judges and common-
wealth's attorneys of the Sixth , and Thirty - eighth Judicial Districts
and creating the offices of circuit judge and commonwealth's at-
torney for the new district established by this Act ; providing for
the holding of court and fixing the time in each district affected
by this Act; and declaring an emergency to exist .
WHEREAS , the General Assembly has given due regard to the elements
of territory , business, and population in the division of the state
into judicial districts ; and
WHEREAS , with such due regard , the General Assembly has found
the following facts to exist :
1. That upon the basis of the entire population of the state , as of
the last preceding federal census , additional judicial districts may be
established to those now existing , pursuant to Sections 128 , 132 , and 138
of the Constitution .
2. That the county of Daviess contains a city of more than 20,000
inhabitants and has a population , including said city , of more than
40,000 , and thus has the privilege , under Section 138 of the Constitution ,
of constituting a judicial district .
3. That due to increased industrial development and the conse-
quent increase in the business of the courts of the counties affected
by this Act , it will expedite the business of the courts and will furnish
needed relief from congested dockets and the delay in the trial of
actions in the counties involved , will be convenient and advantageous
from the standpoint of territory , and will comply with the spirit and
intent of the Constitution with respect to population , to create a new
judicial district to consist of the counties of McLean and Muhlenberg
and to change the Sixth Judicial District so that it will consist of the
county of Daviess only , and the Thirty -eighth Judicial District so that
it will consist of the counties of Butler, Edmonson , Hancock , and Ohio .
NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. There is hereby created the Forty -fifth Judicial
District of Kentucky , which shall be composed of the counties
of McLean and Muhlenberg .
Section 2. The Sixth Judicial District of Kentucky shall
be composed of the county of Daviess only.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to judicial districts
classification.summary: This act creates the Forty-fifth Judicial District in Kentucky, composed of McLean and Muhlenberg counties. It also redefines the Sixth Judicial District to consist only of Daviess county and adjusts the Thirty-eighth Judicial District to include Butler, Edmonson, Hancock, and Ohio counties. The act aims to address increased court business due to industrial development and population changes, ensuring compliance with constitutional provisions regarding judicial districts.
classification.reasoning: The statute's text focuses entirely on the administrative reorganization of judicial districts within Kentucky. It details the creation of a new district and the modification of existing ones based on population, territory, and court business. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for approving and constructing drainage improvements within a metropolitan sewer district. It details procedures for public notice, objection filing, hearings, appeals to county and circuit courts, and final authorization. The process involves review by required agencies, public notification through newspaper publication and posted notices, a period for property owners to file written objections, hearings on objections, and potential resubmission to approving agencies if changes are made. Final decisions are communicated to objectors via registered mail, with further appeal options to the county court, circuit court, and Court of Appeals.

classification.title: An Act relating to metropolitan sewer districts and drainage improvements.
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for classifying land within a metropolitan sewer district for the purpose of assessing costs for drainage improvements. It details criteria for classification (degree of wetness, proximity, soil fertility, surface condition, existing facilities, feasible uses) and establishes three benefit classes (A, B, C) with a corresponding assessment ratio. Impervious areas are designated as Class AA and can be assessed at a higher rate. The act also describes the procedures for preparing and publishing benefit rolls, allowing for public inspection and objections, and conducting hearings.

The classification criteria are based on physical characteristics of the land and its benefit from the improvement, not on race. There is no mention of race or any discriminatory practices. CHAPTER 64 95
WHEREAS , the instruction and training of our youth in the funda-
mentals of learning , as well as in the history , traditions and values
of our state and nation , are essential to the continued well-being
and growth of our people , and to the strength and stability of our
state through the instilling of those cherished tenets of democracy
into the minds and hearts of the youth ; and
WHEREAS , the resolution of these problems and extremities in the
educational system will be found only in the thought and will of
the people ; and
WHEREAS , to determine the sources of these current ills in Kentucky
education , and to determine upon a course for their remedy , a
means and a method for examination and study of the subject must
be afforded to the people of the Commonwealth ; and
WHEREAS , the Governor in his address to the 1956 Session of the
General Assembly on the state of the Commonwealth , did state
and avow ,
Citizens are genuinely concerned with the plight of educa-
tion and are determined that our program be improved and
I intend to dedicate my administration to develop and finance
a complete program of education in Kentucky ... ; " and
..
WHEREAS , the Governor on that occasion did further state ,
"We must continuously study our educational problems ... there-
fore I shall ask the General Assembly for whatever legislation
is necessary to provide for an annual Governor's conference on
education ... to include farmers , labor , business, industry and the
school profession who will study our educational problems and
make plans for the improvement of programs ."
NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. That the Governor of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky be , and hereby is, authorized to cause to be held
annually , in September or October , a conference on education
which shall hereafter be styled the Governor's Conference on
Education .
Section 2. That the Governor is hereby authorized to ap-
point a committee composed of eleven citizens , headed by the
Superintendent of Public Instruction , but not more than five
members of which shall be professional educators ; this commit-
tee to assist the Governor , at his direction , in planning and ar-
ranging for holding the conference , and to recommend to the
Governor persons for appointments as delegates to the con-
ference .
Section 3. That the constituents of such conference shall

CHAPTER 70
(Н. В. 470)
AN ACT changing the classification of the city of Frankfort , Franklin
county , Kentucky , from a city of the third class to a city of the
second class .
WHEREAS , the federal census of 1950 shows a population of 11,916 in
the city of Frankfort , Kentucky ; and
WHEREAS , as a result of territory annexed to the city of Frankfort
since 1950 and the rapid growth in the population of the city of
Frankfort since 1950 , the population of the city of Frankfort has
more than doubled ; and
WHEREAS , the increase since 1950 in electric , water , and gas consumers
and telephone subscribers ; in building permits ; and the number of
persons employed by industry ; and other relevant evidence, clearly
supports the conclusions reported in the 1956 edition of the Economic
and Industrial Survey of the City of Frankfort, Kentucky , prepared
by the Agricultural and Industrial Development Board of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky and the Frankfort Chamber of Commerce ,
establishing the population of the city of Frankfort to now be in
excess or 22,500 ; and
WHEREAS , the foregoing and other relevant evidence has established
the fact, and the General Assembly so finds , that the population of
the city of Frankfort, Kentucky , now exceeds 20,000,
NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
That the city of Frankfort , Franklin county , Kentucky ,
hereby is transferred from a city of the third class to a city of
the second class .

102 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
WHEREAS , the Commonwealth of Kentucky has a requirement for
fourteen National Guard armories and is eligible to receive seventy-
five percent of their construction cost from the federal government ,
provided that the remaining twenty -five percent of construction
cost is made available from state funds .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. This Act may be cited as the National Guard
Armory Construction Act .
Section 2. In enacting this Act , it is the intention of the
General Assembly to provide funds for the construction of
National Guard armories at the following locations : Olive Hill ,
Jackson, Barbourville , Louisville , Shively , Bardstown , Carrollton ,
Brandenburg , Hopkinsville , Tompkinsville , Marion, Monticello,
Middlesboro, and Glasgow .
Section 3. As used in this Act , unless the context other-
wise requires :
( 1 ) "Armory ” means a structure in which to house , ad-
minister , train , and equip a unit or units of the Kentucky Na-
tional Guard, and all appurtenances necessary thereto .
( 2 ) " Federal funds " means funds provided by the Con-
gress of the United States through the enactment of public law
for the construction of National Guard armories in the several
states .
Section 4. To carry out the purposes of this Act , there is
appropriated to the Governor's Special Emergency Fund , for the
purpose of construction of National Guard armories , whatever
surplus remains in the General Expenditure Fund at the end of
the 1956-1957 fiscal year , not to exceed the sum of five hundred
seventy- one thousand dollars . The funds so appropriated shall
be available for the Governor's use for this purpose until ex-
pended , or until the close of the fiscal year 1957-1958 , whichever
occurs first and disbursement of the sum so appropriated shall
be authorized by the Commissioner of Finance on requisition
by the Governor .
Approved February 21 , 1956

AN ACT relating to grounds for divorce .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 403.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amend-
ed to read as follows :
( 1 ) A divorce may be granted to either party for the
following causes :
( a ) Such impotency or malformation as prevents sexual
intercourse ; or
(b ) Living apart without any cohabitation for five con-
secutive years next before application .
( 2) A divorce may be granted to the party not in fault
for the following causes :
( a ) Abandonment for one year ;
(b) Living in adultery ;
( c ) Condemnation for felony in or out of this state ;
( d ) Concealment from the other party of any loathsome
disease existing at the time of marriage , or contracting such a
disease afterward ;
( e ) Force , duress or fraud in obtaining the marriage ; or
( f ) Uniting with any religious society whose creed and
rules require a renunciation of the marriage covenant , or forbid
husband and wife from cohabiting .
( 3) A divorce may be granted to the wife for the following
causes :
( a) When not in like fault, habitual drunkenness on the
part of the husband of not less than one year's duration , accom-
panied by a wasting of the husband's estate and without any
suitable provision for the maintenance of the wife or children ;
( b ) Habitually behaving toward her , for not less than
six months in such cruel and inhuman manner as to indicate a
settled aversion to her or to destroy permanently her peace or
happiness ; or
( c ) Such cruel beating or injury, or attempt at injury,
of the wife as indicates an outrageous temper in the husband ,
or probable danger to her life, or of great bodily injury , from
her remaining with him .

classification.title: AN ACT relating to juries.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 29.045 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning juror eligibility. It states that a juror who has served on the regular panel or as a bystander in circuit court within the preceding twelve months is disqualified. Similarly, a juror who has served in any inferior court within the preceding twelve months is disqualified from serving in any court inferior to the circuit court.

AN ACT relating to the creation of an independent state agency to be
known as the Kentucky Commission on Alcoholism , and the estab-
lishment of a state - wide program for the treatment and rehabilita-
tion of alcoholics , research into the causes and prevention of alco-
holism , and public education concerning this disorder .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
weelth of Kentucky :
Section 1. The purpose of this Act is to establish a state-
wide program for the rehabilitation of alcoholics , research into
the causes and prevention of alcoholism , and public education
concerning the problem of alcoholism .
Section 2. ( 1 ) For the purpose of carrying out the pro-
visions of this Act , there is hereby created a commission to be
hereafter known as the Kentucky Commission on Alcoholism .
The commission shall constitute an agency independent of the
administrative department provided for in Chapter 12 of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes .
( 2 ) The commission shall consist of seven members with
an interest in and knowledge of the alcoholism problem , said
members to be appointed by the Governor . At least two of the
seven members shall be doctors , and one of the doctors shall be a
psychiatrist . Three members shall be appointed for a term of one
year , two members shall be appointed for a term of two years
and two members shall be appointed for a term of three years .
At the expiration of these terms appointments shall be for terms
of four years . Appointments shall be made by the Governor to
fill any existing vacancies .
( 3 ) The duties of the commission shall be to study the
problems of alcoholism within the state , to formulate and carry
out programs of education designed to curb the excessive use of
alcohol and reduce the number of alcoholics , to establish such
facilities and employ such personnel as are necessary and avail-
able for the diagnosis and rehabilitation of alcoholics , to accept

classification.title: Section 205.220 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows
classification.summary: This section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes outlines eligibility requirements for public assistance. It specifies that public assistance is not payable to inmates of public institutions (except as patients in medical institutions), or to individuals in institutions for tuberculosis or mental diseases, or those diagnosed with tuberculosis or psychosis who are patients in medical institutions. It also allows the commissioner to require school attendance for needy children and to prescribe the degree of relationship for the child's caregiver. Furthermore, it states that children born out of wedlock after June 15, 1950, are not eligible for aid until a bastardy proceeding is initiated, with provisions for waiving this requirement. The department must notify law enforcement if aid is provided to a child deserted or abandoned by a parent. Finally, it states that needy aged, blind, child, or permanently and totally disabled persons must be residents of the state for a period prescribed by regulation to be eligible for public assistance.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns eligibility for public assistance programs, including aid to dependent children, the aged, blind, and disabled. It sets conditions related to institutionalization, school attendance, illegitimacy, and residency. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The conditions apply universally to all individuals seeking public assistance, regardless of their racial background. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 205.240 concerning public assistance funds
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 205.240 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, detailing the appropriation and allocation of funds for public assistance programs. It specifies that all state, federal, and other funds for public assistance are appropriated to the commissioner for expenditure. Prior to July 1st of each fiscal year, the commissioner must allocate these funds to four categories: needy children, needy aged, needy permanently and totally disabled persons, and needy blind, based on estimated numbers of applicants and recipients. The commissioner also has the discretion to reallocate funds during a legislative biennium.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the State Racing Commission.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 230.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the State Racing Commission. It establishes the commission as an independent agency, outlines the appointment process for its seven members (requiring three to be breeders and no two from the same racing association), and sets their term at four years. The commission is also required to submit a biennial report to the General Assembly.

CHAPTER 77 117
(3 ) All relations of dependency referred to in this section
shall mean dependency existing at the time of the accident to
the employe or at the time his disability from an occupational

(4) If death occurs as a result of the injury or an occu-
pational disease after a period of total or partial disability , the
period of disability shall be deducted from the total period of
compensation and the benefits paid thereunder from the maxi-
mum allowed for death by this section .
Section 6. Section 342.095 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) When the injury or occupational disease causes total
disability for work, the employer , during such disability , except
the first seven days thereof , shall pay the employe a weekly
compensation equal to 65 percent of his average weekly earn-
ings , not to exceed $32 nor less than $ 12 per week , such pay-
ments to be made during the period of total disability but not
longer than 425 weeks after the date of the injury , or after the
date his disability from an occupational disease began, nor in
any case to exceed a maximum sum of $13,600 . If the period
of total disability begins after a period of partial disability , the
period of partial disability shall be deducted from the total
period of 425 weeks during which compensation for total dis-
ability may be payable , and the payments made on account of
such partial disability shall be deducted from the maximum of
$ 13,600 .
(2 ) In case of the following injuries or disabilities from
occupational disease the disability shall be considered total and
permanent :
( a ) The total permanent loss of sight in both eyes .
(b) The loss of both feet at or above the ankle .
(c ) The loss of both hands at or above the wrist .
(d ) A similar loss of one hand and one foot .
( e ) A similar loss of one hand and one eye .
(f ) A similar loss of one foot and one eye.
( g ) A disability to the spine, resulting in permanent and
complete paralysis of both arms or both legs, or of one arm and
one leg.

It details the calculation of weekly compensation based on average weekly earnings, maximum benefit periods, and minimum/maximum weekly amounts. For temporary partial disability, compensation is 65% of the difference between pre-injury and current earnings, capped at $27/week and $9,500 total. For enumerated injuries (like loss of fingers), compensation is 65% of average weekly wages for a set number of weeks, with weekly limits between $12 and $26.

It clarifies the process for dependents of a deceased employee to receive unpaid disability compensation awards. The amendment specifies the requirements for filing a claim, the notification process for the awarding party, and the determination of dependency. It also sets limits on the total amount paid to the decedent and dependents, and a maximum weekly payment to dependents.

classification.title: Amendment to Section 342.316 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 342.316 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning occupational diseases. It defines an occupational disease as one arising out of and in the course of employment, excluding ordinary diseases of life unless they are an incident of an occupational disease. The amendment clarifies the criteria for a disease to be deemed arising out of employment, requiring a direct causal connection to the work conditions and exposure, and not from a hazard equally exposed to the general public outside of employment. It also outlines provisions for medical examinations, fees, and travel expenses for employees.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 342.315 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes Relating to Occupational Diseases
classification.summary: This statute amends the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning occupational diseases, specifically silicosis. It outlines conditions for compensation eligibility, including the duration of exposure, the timing of compensation payments, and provisions for cases complicated by tuberculosis. It also details conditions under which compensation would not be payable, such as false representations by the employee regarding previous disability or health status. The amendment focuses on the procedural and eligibility aspects of workers' compensation for occupational diseases.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses occupational diseases, specifically silicosis, and outlines the conditions for compensation under the Workmen's Compensation Act. It details requirements for exposure, payment timing, and conditions under which compensation might be denied, such as misrepresentation of prior health conditions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute pertains to labor and workers' rights in general, not to racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

1 ( 9 ) Where an occupational disease is aggravated by other

( 11 ) Whenever any claimant misconceives his remedy
and files an application for adjustment of claim under the gen-
eral provisions of this Act and it is subsequently discovered , at
any time before the final disposition of such cause , that the claim
for injury , disability or death which was the basis for such ap-
plication should properly have been made under the provisions
of this section, then the application so filed may be amended in
form or substance , or both , to assert a claim for such injury, dis-
ability or death under the provisions of this section, and it shall
be deemed to have been so filed as amended on the date of the
original filing thereof , and such compensation may be awarded
as is warranted by the whole evidence pursuant to the provisions
of this Act . When such amendment is submitted , further or
additional evidence may be heard by the board when deemed
necessary . Nothing this section contains shall be construed to
be or permit a waiver of any of the provisions of this Act with
reference to notice of time for filing a claim , but notice of filing
a claim, if given or done , shall be deemed to be a notice of filing
of a claim under provisions of this Act , if given or done within
the time required herein .

classification.title: CHAPTER 77
classification.summary: This statute outlines provisions for compensation related to occupational diseases. It details how compensation should be reduced if an occupational disease is aggravated by a non-compensable condition, or if a non-compensable condition is aggravated by an occupational disease. It also specifies that compensation for death from an occupational disease is not payable to individuals whose relationship to the deceased arose after the beginning of the first compensable disability, with an exception for after-born children of a marriage existing at that time. Finally, it allows for amendment of claims filed under general provisions to be treated as filed under these specific occupational disease provisions, without waiving notice requirements.

CHAPTER 84
(S. B. 172)
AN ACT relating to state universities and colleges .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 164.130 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
The government of the University of Kentucky is vested
in a board of trustees consisting of the Governor , who shall
be chairman of the board , the Superintendent of Public In-
struction , the Commissioner of Agriculture , Labor and Statis-
tics, and twelve competent citizens of Kentucky appointed by
the Governor . The terms of appointed members shall be for
four years and until their successors are appointed and qualify.
Three of the twelve appointed members shall be appointed
each year . Three of the appointed members shall be repre-
sentative of agricultural interests, three shall be alumni of the
university and six shall be other distinguished citizens of the
state . The Governor shall so make the appointments as to
divide the representation upon the board , including the ex of-
ficio members , as nearly equally as possible between the two
leading political parties of this state. Appointments to fill va-
cancies shall be made for the unexpired term in the same man-
ner as provided for original appointments .

AN ACT relating to the Public Service Commission , amending sections
278.050 , 278.060 and 278.120 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 278.050 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows :
(1) The Public Service Commission shall consist of three
members appointed by the Governor with the advice and consent
of the Senate . If the Senate is not in session when a term expires
or a vacancy occurs , the Governor shall make the appointment to
take effect at once, subject to the approval of the Senate when
convened . Each of the three members of said commission shall
be appointed on or before March 1 , 1956 , for a term of four
years and until a successor is appointed and qualified .
(2) The Governor shall appoint one of the commissioners to
act as chairman of the commission .
(3) Vacancies for unexpired terms shall be filled in the
same manner as original appointments , but the appointee shall
hold only to the end of the unexpired term .
Section 2. Section 278.060 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows :
(1) Each commissioner shall be a resident and qualified
voter of this state , not less than twenty -five years of age at the
time of his appointment and qualification , and shall have resided
in this state for at least three years prior to his appointment and
qualification . Not more than two of the commissioners shall be

classification.title: An Act relating to the establishment and operation of pay lakes.
classification.summary: This act establishes regulations for the creation and operation of pay lakes in Kentucky. It outlines requirements for stocking, licensing fees based on acreage, and the issuance of permits to patrons. The act also specifies conditions under which a fisherman might be exempt from needing a special pay lake permit, such as already possessing a state-wide or non-resident fishing license, or a ten-day license used within its validity period. It also grants the commissioner of the Department of Fish and Wild Life Resources the authority to revoke operator licenses for non-compliance.

The regulations appear to be purely administrative and economic, related to the management of recreational fishing facilities. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend Section 256 of the Constitution of this Commonwealth relating to the amendment of said Constitution.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 256 of the Kentucky Constitution, detailing the process for proposing and adopting constitutional amendments. It outlines that amendments can be proposed in either house of the General Assembly during regular or special sessions, requiring a three-fifths majority vote in each house. The proposed amendments, along with the yeas and nays, are to be entered in the journals and then submitted to the voters.

Section 2. ( 1 ) Any circuit court , subject to the provisions
and conditions provided in this Act, may postpone the entering
of judgment and the imposing of sentence and probate any per-
son arraigned before it and charged with crime . The court
shall not postpone the entering of judgment or the imposing of
sentence on any person whose punishment is fixed by the jury
at life imprisonment or death . Except in cases where punishment
is life imprisonment or death , circuit courts after a plea of
guilty or after the returning of a verdict of guilty by the jury ,
may postpone the entering of judgment and the sentencing of
the defendant and place the defendant on probation , or may
impose a fine and also place the defendant on probation .
( 2 ) In placing any defendant on probation the court shall
direct that such defendant be under the supervision of the di-
vision of probation and parole .
Section 3. The period of probation shall be fixed by the
court and at any time may be extended or shortened by the
court by order duly entered . Such period , with any extensions
thereof , shall not exceed five years , except in cases in which the
defendant is charged with failure to support his dependents .
Upon completion of the probationary period the defendant shall
be deemed finally discharged , provided , no warrant issued by
the court is pending against him, and probation has not been
revoked .
Section 4. ( 1 ) No defendant convicted of or who has
pleaded guilty to a felony shall be placed on probation until a
written report of investigation by a probation officer shall have
been presented to and considered by the court .
( 2) The court in any case to assist it in determining
whether probation should be considered may order an investi-
gation by the probation officer.
( 3 ) The probation officer shall inquire into the circum-
stances of the offense , criminal record , social history and present
condition of the defendant . Whenever indicated and practicable

classification.title: An Act relating to probation and parole
classification.summary: This act allows circuit courts to postpone judgment and sentencing for individuals charged with crimes, placing them on probation. It outlines the conditions for probation, including supervision by the division of probation and parole, a maximum probation period of five years (with exceptions for failure to support dependents), and requirements for pre-probation investigation reports for felony convictions. The court can extend or shorten the probation period. A defendant is discharged upon completion of probation, provided no warrants are pending and probation has not been revoked.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the postponement of judgment and the imposition of sentence, and the placement of defendants on probation. It outlines the procedures and conditions for probation, including investigations and supervision. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1956.

Section 5. By order duly entered the court may impose ,
and at any time may modify , any conditions of probation . The
court shall cause a copy of such order to be delivered to the
probation officer and the probationer . It may impose as a con-
dition , that the probationer shall do any of the following or any
other :
( 1 ) Avoid injurious or vicious habits ;
( 2 ) Avoid persons or places of disreputable or harmful
character ;
( 3 ) Report to the probation officer as directed ;
(4) Permit the probation officer to visit him at his home
or elsewhere ;
( 5 ) Work faithfully at suitable employment as far as
possible ;
( 6 ) Remain within a specified area ;
(7) Pay such an amount of money into the court imposing
the conditions as directed by that court ;
( 8 ) Make reparation or restitution , in an amount to be
determined by the court , to an aggrieved party for damage or
loss caused by his offense ;
(9 ) Support his dependents .
Section 6. ( 1 ) At any time during probation the court
may issue a warrant for the violation of any of the conditions of
probation and cause the probationer to be arrested . Any pro-
bation officer having reason to believe that a probationer has
violated his probation may arrest him without a warrant or may
deputize any peace officer to do so by giving him a written
statement setting forth that the probationer , in the judgment
of said probation officer , has violated the conditions of proba-
tion. Such written statement delivered with the probationer
to the officer in charge of a county jail or other place of detention
shall be sufficient warrant for the detention of the probationer .
The probation officer shall immediately notify the court of the
arrest and detention of the probationer and shall submit in
writing a report showing in what manner the probationer has
violated his probation . Thereupon , or upon arrest by warrant
as herein provided , the court shall cause the defendant to be

( 3 ) The court without a hearing may revoke the pro-

Section 7. The commissioner , with the approval of the
Governor , shall appoint a director of the division of probation
and parole , who , with the approval of the commissioner , shall
appoint a number of probation , parole and conditional release
officers and other employees sufficient to administer the pro-
visions of this Act ; but no employee shall be appointed except
in the manner hereinafter provided . The director shall be a
person having attained at least a bachelor's degree from an
accredited college and in addition thereto shall be a person with
training and experience in probation , parole or other related
form of welfare work.
Section 8. ( 1 ) The Governor with the advice of the com-
missioner shall appoint a parole board consisting of three mem-
bers , one of whom he shall name as chairman of the board . The
board shall be attached to the department of welfare . Persons
named to the board by the Governor shall be those who have
demonstrated their knowledge and experience in correctional
treatment or crime prevention and shall be appointed without
regard to their political affiliation .
( 2 ) The members of the board shall give full time to the
duties of their office and shall receive necessary traveling ex-
penses and not less than $7,500.00 per annum compensation ,
except the chairman of the board shall receive additional com-
pensation of $ 500.00 per year for his services . Their terms of
office shall be four years and until their successors are appointed
and have qualified , except that the first three members shall be
appointed for terms of two, three , and four years , respectively .

rant and authority to the officer in charge of any state penal

It also details the process for employee appointments through competitive examinations conducted by the division of personnel efficiency, with provisions for waiving certain requirements for existing employees. Furthermore, it establishes that no employee, except for the commissioner and director, shall be discharged except for cause after the act's effective date. Section 26 specifically prohibits probation, parole, or conditional release officers of the division from engaging in political activities such as influencing elections, serving on campaign committees, interfering with elections, or soliciting political contributions.

162 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Section 31. ( 1 ) The Governor of this state is hereby
authorized and directed to execute a compact on behalf of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky with any of the United States
legally joining therein in the form substantially as follows :
A COMPАСТ
Entered into by and among the contracting states , signa-
tories hereto with the consent of the Congress of the United
States of America , granted by an Act entitled "An Act granting
the consent of Congress to any two or more states to enter into
agreements or compacts for cooperative effort and mutual as-
sistance in the prevention of crime and for other purposes . " The
contracting states solemnly agree :
Article I
It shall be competent for the duly constituted judicial and
administrative authorities of a state party to this compact
(herein called "sending state " ) , to permit any person convicted
of an offense within such state and placed on probation or re-
leased on parole to reside in any other state party to this com-
pact ( herein called "receiving state " ) , while on probation or
parole , if ( a ) such person is in fact a resident of or has his
family residing within the receiving state and can obtain employ-
ment there ; or ( b ) though not a resident of the receiving state
and not having his family residing there , the receiving state
consents to such person being sent there .
Before granting such permission , opportunity shall be
granted to the receiving state to investigate the home and pros-
pective employment of such person .
A resident of the receiving state , within the meaning of
this section , is one who has been an actual inhabitant of such
state continuously for more than one year prior to his coming
to the sending state and has not resided within the sending state
more than six continuous months immediately preceding the
commission of the offense for which he has been convicted .
Article II
Each receiving state will assume the duties of visitation of
and supervision over probationers or parolees of any sending
state and in the exercise of those duties will be governed by the
same standards that prevail for
itsown probationers and parolees
.

classification.title: An Act to Authorize the Governor to Execute a Compact on Behalf of the Commonwealth of Kentucky with Any of the United States Legally Joining Therein.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Governor of Kentucky to enter into a compact with other states for the interstate supervision of probationers and parolees. The compact allows for individuals convicted of offenses and placed on probation or parole to reside in another signatory state if they are residents of that state, have family there, can obtain employment, or if the receiving state consents. The receiving state agrees to assume visitation and supervision duties under its own standards.

CHAPTER 104
(Н. В. 156)
AN ACT relating to establishment of Boonesboro State Park .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. There shall be a State Park at the site of the
original Fort Boonesboro in Madison county , Kentucky , which
park shall be known as Boonesboro State Park. This park shall
consist of no more than 200 acres and shall be developed for
historical and/or recreational purposes at the discretion of the
Commissioner of Conservation , in commemoration of the his-
torical significance of Fort Boonesboro to the history and tra-
ditions of the Commonwealth .
Section 2. The division of parks is hereby authorized , in
the discretion of the Commissioner of Conservation , to acquire
real property on and near the site of the original Fort Boonesboro
in accordance with the provisions of KRS 148.060 . The division
of parks is further authorized to provide for the maintenance of
Boonesboro State Park in the manner provided by law .
Approved February 24 , 1956

(3 ) The retail installment contract shall contain a definite
statement in twelve point bold type or larger , that the insurance ,
if any, included in the retail installment sale provides or does not
provide coverage for personal liability and property damage
caused to others , as the case may be .
(4) The amount, if any, included for insurance , shall not
exceed the premiums chargeable in accordance with applicable
rate filings made with the Commissioner of Insurance . Every
retail seller or sales finance company , if insurance on the motor
vehicle is included in a retail installment contract shall within
thirty days after execution of the retail installment contract send
or cause to be sent to the retail buyer a policy or policies or cer-
tificate of insurance , which insurance shall be written by a com-
pany authorized to do business in this state , clearly setting forth
the amount of the premium , the kind or kinds of insurance and
the scope of the coverage and all the terms , exceptions , limita-
tions , restrictions and conditions of the contract or contracts of
the insurance . The buyer of a motor vehicle under a retail
installment contract shall have the privilege of purchasing such
insurance from an agent or broker of his own selection and of
selecting an insurance company acceptable to the seller ; pro-
vided , however , that the inclusion of the insurance premium in
the retail installment contract when the buyer selects the agent,
broker or company, shall be optional with the seller .
If any such policy is cancelled , the unearned insurance pre-
mium refund received by the holder of the contract shall be
credited to the final maturing installments of the retail install-
ment contract .
(5) Any sales finance company hereunder may purchase or
acquire from any retail seller any retail installment contract on
such terms and conditions as may be agreed upon between them.

classification.title: Classification of Teachers
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 157.390 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to establish a system for classifying public school teachers into seven ranks based on their educational qualifications, specifically the type and number of college semester hours earned, and whether they hold a master's degree. It outlines the criteria for each rank, from Rank I (master's degree plus 24 graduate hours) down to Rank VII (fewer than 32 college hours). The Superintendent of Public Instruction, under State Board of Education regulations, is responsible for this classification.

classification.title: An Act to amend KRS 157.400 relating to the public school foundation program fund.
classification.summary: This act amends KRS 157.400 to adjust the distribution of funds from the public school foundation program. classification.title: AN ACT relating to license fee revenue for certain boards of education.
classification.summary: This act allows boards of education in counties containing a city of the first class to impose license fees on businesses, trades, occupations, and professions for revenue purposes. The fees can be up to one-half of one percent of salaries, wages, commissions, and other compensations earned within the school district, and the net profits of businesses and professions from activities conducted within the district. The imposition of these fees requires approval from a majority of voters in a school district election.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the financial responsibilities for elections held by boards of education and details the imposition and collection of license fees by these boards. It specifies that companies paying ad valorem and franchise taxes are exempt from license fees, as are insurance companies, banks, trust companies, and combined banking/trust businesses. The statute also grants boards of education the authority to collect these fees directly or through contracted agencies, including the power to require employers to withhold fees at the source, set due dates, and implement penalties for overdue payments. Proceeds from these fees are to be remitted to the respective boards of education.

It also details the requirement for employees to file a statement of service history to be eligible for benefits.

Section 18. Service Retirement Allowance .
( 1 ) Upon service retirement at normal retirement date ,
or subsequent thereto , a member shall receive an annual retire-
ment allowance , payable monthly during his lifetime , which shall
consist of an amount equal to 4 percent of the first $ 1,320 of final
compensation multiplied by the number of years of service, plus
1 percent of the next $ 2,880 of final compensation multiplied by
the number of years of service , plus 14 percent of final compen-
sation in excess of $4,200 multiplied by the number of years of
service .
(2 ) Upon service retirement prior to normal retire-
ment date , a member shall receive an annual retirement
allowance payable monthly during his lifetime which shall be
determined in the same manner as for retirement at his normal
retirement date with years of service and final compensation
being determined as of the date of his actual retirement , but the
amount of the retirement allowance so determined shall be re-
duced by 5/12 percent for each 30 days that the actual re-
tirement date precedes the normal retirement date .
Section 19. Disability Retirement .
Any member in service who has fifteen or more years of
service may, at any time before his normal retirement date , re-

compensation on the date of disability continued in the same
amount up to his normal retirement date and that years of service
include the period of disability retirement prior to his normal
retirement date .

Section 23. Cessation of Disability Retirement Allowance .

allowance recover from such disability at any time prior to his
normal retirement date , his disability retirement allowance shall

tirement allowance was computed shall thereafter be counted
as service and , in addition , the period of disability retirement
shall be counted as service .

such member as of the date of disability retirement over the
disability retirement allowances received by him shall be trans-
ferred from the retirement allowance account to the members '
contribution account .

classification.summary: This section outlines the procedures for paying lump sum death benefits under the Act. Payments are to be made to a designated beneficiary if one has been nominated in writing and filed with the board. If no beneficiary is designated, the payment will go to the deceased member's executors or administrators. The nomination can be changed by the member or retired member at any time through written designation.

It details how beneficiaries are designated and how payments are made upon the death of a member or retired member. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by '198 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY'.
