CHAPTER 171 345
( 1) If any motor carrier gasoline user neglects or refuses
to make the return or pay the tax at the time provided in KRS
138.685 , a penalty of 20 percent of the tax and interest of 6 per-
cent per annum from the due date of the tax on both the tax
and the penalty shall be paid.
( 2 ) If any motor carrier subject to the penalty provided in
subsection ( 1 ) of this section submits to the department in
writing the reasons for failure to comply with KRS 138.650 to
138.725 and if the department finds the reason sufficient evi-
dence to justify modifying the penalty provided in subsection
( 1 ) of this section, it may modify the penalty enacted therein
to 5 percent of the amount of tax due and delinquent , provided
the 5 percent penalty may be reduced to a one percent penalty
if the violation is the first violation by the taxpayer within the
preceding twelve months .
(3 ) If the penalty provided by this section is collected by
proceedings in court , an additional penalty of 20 percent shall
be collected and distributed as authorized by KRS 134.400 .
Whenever any motor carrier neglects or refuses to make and file
any return for any quarter as required by KRS 138.685 , or files
an incorrect or fraudulent return , the department shall determine
after an investigation the number of gallons of gasoline with re-
spect to which the motor carrier has incurred liability under
KRS 138.660 and 138.690 for any particular quarter and assess
and collect the amount of tax and penalties due .
(4) Any motor carrier who fails to make any return re-
quired under the provisions of KRS 138.650 to 138.725 within
the time allowed may be required to pay a penalty of $ 10 to be
assessed and collected in the manner provided for the assess-
ment and collection of taxes , or may be proceeded against in a
civil action instigated by the department . In addition , such
delinquent motor carrier may be compelled to make the required
return .
( 5) In any action for the collection of gasoline use taxes
and any penalties or interest imposed in connection therewith ,
an assessment by the department of the amount of tax due and
the interest or penalties due to the state shall constitute prima
facie evidence of the claim of the state and the burden of proof
shall be on the motor carrier to show that the assessment was in-
correct or contrary to law .

CHAPTER 172
(Н. В. 482 )
AN ACT relating to the rehabilitation of the handicapped .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
monwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. In enacting this legislation it is the intention
of the General Assembly of Kentucky to provide for and improve
the rehabilitation of the physically and mentally handicapped
citizens of the Commonwealth of Kentucky in order that they
may increase their social and economic well-being , and the
productive capacity of the Commonwealth and nation .
Section 2. The Superintendent of Public Instruction is
hereby authorized to establish within the Department of Educa-
tion a State Rehabilitation Agency . This agency shall have
such powers and duties as contained in this Act and such other
functions as may be established by regulation of the State Board
of Education .
Section 3. Rehabilitation services shall be provided di-
rectly or through public or private instrumentalities to any
handicapped individual ( 1 ) who is a citizen of the Common-
wealth at the time of filing his application therefor , and whose
rehabilitation the State Rehabilitation Agency determines after
full investigation can be satisfactorily achieved , or ( 2 ) who is
eligible therefor under the terms of an agreement with another
state or with the federal government .

classification.title: An Act relating to the ascertainment of income and principal and the apportionment of receipts and expenses between tenants and remaindermen.
classification.summary: This act defines terms such as 'principal', 'income', 'tenant', 'remainderman', and 'trustee' in the context of trusts and estates. It establishes rules for how income and principal are determined and how receipts and expenses are allocated between those who receive current benefits (tenants) and those who will ultimately receive the principal (remaindermen). The act applies to situations where a principal has been established, with or without a trust, and allows the person establishing the principal to make specific provisions that override the act's general rules, provided they are not contrary to law.
classification.reasoning: The statute defines terms related to trusts and estates, specifically how income and principal are to be ascertained and how receipts and expenses are to be apportioned between tenants and remaindermen. There is no mention of race, and the subject matter is purely financial and legal concerning property distribution. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

principal shall enure to or fall upon principal .
(3) All income after payment of expenses properly charge-
able to it shall be paid and delivered to the tenant or retained by

legally so directed by the terms of the transaction by which the
principal was established; while the principal shall be held for

action by which it was established or by law.
Section 4. Apportionment of Income . Whenever a tenant
shall have the right to income from periodic payments , which
shall include rent , interest on loans , and annuities , but shall not
include dividends on corporate shares , and such right shall cease
and determine by death or in any other manner at a time other
than the date when such periodic payments should be paid , he or
his personal representative shall be entitled to that portion of any
such income next payable which amounts to the same percentage
thereof as the time elapsed from the last due date of such periodic
payments to and including the day of the determination of his
right is of the total period during which such income would
normally accrue . The remaining income shall be paid to the per-
son next entitled to income by the terms of the transaction by
which the principal was established . But no action shall be
brought by the trustee or tenant to recover such apportioned
income or any portion thereof until after the day on which it

CHAPTER 181
(S. R. 12)
A CONCURRENT RESOLUTION memorializing Congress to restore
tobacco acreage allotments to their 1955 level .
WHEREAS , agriculture is of highest importance to the economy of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky ;
WHEREAS , the production of tobacco is of first importance in the
agriculture of the Commonwealth ;
WHEREAS , there has been drastic reduction of tobacco acreage over the
past two years totaling about forty percent , resulting in considerable
reduction in total farm production and causing hardship to the
farmers in this Commonwealth ;
WHEREAS , the total 1955 tobacco production has fallen far below the
earlier crop estimates on which the 1956 allotments were based ;
WHEREAS , there has been favorable increase in consumption of tobacco
over the past several months ;
WHEREAS , it now appears that reductions in tobacco acreage allot-
ments for 1956, made in November of 1955 , are unwise and unneces-
sary ;
WHEREAS , the United States Department of Agriculture is without
authority to now correct its 1956 allotments to adjust to the several
differences between the estimates and the facts ; and
WHEREAS , failure to do everything possible to bring about the pro-
duction of tobacco in amounts most suitable to the economy of
tobacco marketing within the principles of the production control
program of the federal government , would undermine confidence in
the program and cause undue hardship on the farmers of the
Commonwealth ;
NOW , THEREFORE ,

CHAPTER 183
(S. R. 22 )
A CONCURRENT RESOLUTION memorializing Congress to establish
an inland naval training center on Lake Cumberland .
WHEREAS , it is in the interest of the peace of the world that the United
States maintain military forces sufficient to defend against any ag-
gression threatening the security of America ;
WHEREAS , essential to such military forces is the development of naval
forces familiar with every kind of situation , and development of
such forces in every part of the country to enable each part to mount
a complete defense in case other parts are rendered inoperative as a
consequence of attack or subversion ;
WHEREAS , there is at this time no complete center for training in naval
science and tactics in the Southeast Central part of the United States
and this in spite of the presence of many natural major bodies of
water and the development of many of the world's largest artificial
bodies of water ;
WHEREAS , the Lake Cumberland Region offers an ideal situation for
institutional endeavor in that it affords a mild temperate climate
with a normal amount of rainfall and a sample of nearly every kind
of terrain and weather conditions , and sets amid beautiful scenery ,
deep in pioneer tradition and the social graces of hospitable living;
WHEREAS , the Lake Cumberland area is an ideal location for serving
Eastern and Central United States in that it is centrally located in
relationship to the eastern half of the United States , to the Ohio
River Valley, and to the great inland basin of the United States and
enjoys a regional proximity to more states than any other state ,

CHAPTER 188 369
WHEREAS , in 1946 that section was repealed and KRS 158.030 was
amended to provide that any child who was six years of age on or
before January 1 following the opening of the school year could
enter school at the beginning of the school year , except in schools
having mid -year promotions ; and
WHEREAS , in 1950 the aforesaid statute was amended to provide that
any child who was six years of age on or before September 1 fol-
lowing the opening of the school year could enter school at or
within thirty days of the beginning of the school year ; and
WHEREAS , in 1952 the aforesaid statute was again amended to provide
that any child who is six years of age on or before December 30
following the opening of school may enter school ; and
WHEREAS , in a period of less than ten years , the statute dealing with
entrance age of children entering the common schools has been
amended four times and each amendment has affected hundreds
of Kentucky's children and their parents , as well as many teachers
in the elementary schools of the state ; and
WHEREAS , a study on the question would appear desirable ,
NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common-
monwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That the Legislative Research Commission is
authorized and directed to make a careful study of the question
of what should be the entrance age of pupils entering the com-
mon schools . In making such study the Legislative Research
Commission should explore the possibility of requiring pre - school
examinations .
Section 2. That the Legislative Research Commission shall
report to the next regular session of the General Assembly
its
findings and recommendations with respect
to
itsstudy
.
Approved February
27, 1956

classification.summary: This act extends the time for a joint committee, established by Senate Resolution No. 6, to complete its investigation and report. The committee is granted an additional twenty days beyond the adjournment of the 1956 Regular Session, excluding Sundays and holidays. It is tasked with proceeding rapidly, making a preliminary report to the Governor, Speaker of the House, and President of the Senate, and a final report at a special or extraordinary session in 1956. Committee members will be paid $25 per day for services and $10 per day for living expenses between sessions, and reimbursed for actual expenses. Members are authorized to meet and conduct business even when the General Assembly is not in session. Financial obligations will be paid as per House Bill No. 3 of the 1956 Regular Session.

CHAPTER 193
(H. R. 14)
A JOINT RESOLUTION directing the Legislative Research Commission
to conduct a study on the points and demerits system of handling
traffic violations .
WHEREAS , the death toll of victims of accidents on the highways of the
United States during the past year exceeds the total number of
casualties during the three years of the Korean War ;
WHEREAS , the National Safety Council advises that most traffic acci-
dents are the direct result of violations of traffic laws and regula-
tions ; and
WHEREAS , the present methods of handling traffic violations seem
inadequate to reduce the frequency of violations ;
NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common-
monwealth of Kentucky :
That the Legislative Research Commission is directed to
make a study of the value of the points and demerits system
of controlling traffic violations under which violations are
evaluated numerically and totaled providing quantum meas-
urements for determining whether a violator's operator's license
should be suspended or revoked .
Approved February 27, 1956

CHAPTER 194

A JOINT RESOLUTION authorizing K. V. Clark to sue the Department
of Welfare , Kentucky State Reformatory and the Commonwealth of
Kentucky or either of them .
WHEREAS , the Commonwealth of Kentucky through the Department
of Welfare operates and controls the Kentucky State Reformatory
at La Grange , Oldham county , Kentucky , one of its purposes being
for the confinement and guarding of prisoners of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky , and
WHEREAS , on or about February 1, 1955 K. V. Clark of Jefferson coun-
ty, was an employe of the Commonwealth of Kentucky and as such
employe was rendering , on or about said February 1 , 1955, service
to the Commonwealth of Kentucky by performing his duties as a
receiver at the Kentucky State Reformatory at La Grange , Oldham
county , Kentucky , and
WHEREAS , the said K. V. Clark, while regularly engaged in his duties
as a receiver at the Kentucky State Reformatory , La Grange , Oldham
county , Kentucky , was , the said K. V. Clark alleges , injured serious-
ly and permanently when a stack of beds , which he was inspecting ,
fell upon him , as a result of negligence of the Kentucky State Re-
formatory , Department of Welfare and the employes and agents of
the Commonwealth of Kentucky in their failure to furnish him a
safe place in which to work , and
WHEREAS , the said K. V. Clark alleges his injuries and damages are in
an amount greater than five thousand dollars , and therefore ade-
quate remedy cannot be had by resort to action before the Board
of Claims pursuant to the provisions of KRS 44.070 .
NOW , THEREFORE , in order that the said K. V. Clark may have his
day in court to determine his rights by judicial action and the ques-
tion of negligence , if any, causing said injury .
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common-
monwealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That K. V. Clark of Louisville , Jefferson county ,
Kentucky , be and is hereby authorized and permitted to sue
the Commonwealth of Kentucky , the Department of Welfare
and the Kentucky State Reformatory at La Grange or any one
or more of them , in the circuit court of Jefferson county or the
circuit court of Franklin county for such damages as he suf-
fered and sustained as a direct and proximate result of the
negligence , if any, of the employes and agents of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky , the Department of Welfare and the
Kentucky State Reformatory at La Grange .

CHAPTER 196
(H. R. 24)
A JOINT RESOLUTION providing for a study and report on the question
of establishing city and county governmental authority over, and
responsibility for, purely local problems , and relieving the General
Assembly of such duties .
WHEREAS , local governmental units at the city and county level are
confronted with many varied and complex problems , which are best
and more economically resolved at the local level ; and
WHEREAS , under the provisions of our present Constitution and statutes ,
the General Assembly must continually concern itself with matters
which are purely local in their nature , thus making it impossible for
the legislature to give that time and attention to matters of state-
wide importance , which is its proper function ; and
WHEREAS , it is desirable , for these purposes , to establish the authority
and responsibility of the people's duly elected and appointed city and
county officials over such matters; and

classification.title: AN ACT relating to disability insurance.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 304.715 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning disability insurance. It introduces a provision that after three years from the policy's issue date, no misstatements (except fraudulent ones) made by the applicant in the application shall be used to void the policy or deny a claim for loss or disability commencing after that three-year period. This provision does not affect legal requirements for avoidance or denial during the initial three-year period, nor does it limit the application of KRS 304.731 to 304.735 in cases of misstatement regarding age, occupation, or other insurance.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an amendment to the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning disability insurance. It establishes a 'time limit on certain defenses' for insurance policies, specifying that after three years, misstatements in the application cannot be used to void the policy or deny claims, with exceptions for fraudulent misstatements and specific types of misrepresentations (age, occupation, other insurance). There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply universally to all policyholders. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

There is no mention of race, and the provisions appear to apply generally to all landowners within the district without any discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Section 16. Tax funds collected shall be transferred to and
held by the treasurer of the watershed conservancy district for
the specific purpose for which they have been collected . All
expenditures of such funds shall be made by the board of direc-
tors upon order of the board , approved by the board of super-
visors of the soil conservation district or districts involved .
Section 17. ( 1) Any one or more owners of land may
petition the board of supervisors to have their lands added to a
watershed conservancy district . Such petition shall define the
boundaries of the land desired to be annexed, the number of
acres of land involved , and other information pertinent to such
proposal . When the boundary described embraces lands of others
than the petitioners , the petition shall so state and shall be signed
by twenty -five or more of the landowners in the territory de-
scribed , if fifty or more such owners are involved , or by a majority
if less than fifty landowners are involved .
(2) Within thirty days after such petition is filed, the board
shall cause due notice to be given , as provided in KRS 262.010
(4) , of a hearing on such petition . All interested parties shall have
a right to attend such hearing and be heard . The board shall
determine whether the lands described in the petition or any
portion thereof shall be included in the district . If all the land-
owners in the territory involved are not petitioners , a referendum
shall be held within such territory as provided in sections 6,
7, and 8 of this Act , before making a final determination . If it is

classification.summary: This act amends Section 64.190 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to specify the fees that constables may collect for various services, including making arrests for motor vehicle violations on public highways, serving summons in civil cases before a justice of the peace, and serving peace or search warrants. It also includes provisions related to loan limits and restrictions for companies organized under KRS 291.120, and penalties for violations.

The second section (Chapter 205) is an act that amends KRS 64.190 to outline the fees constables can charge for specific services like arrests and serving warrants. Neither section contains any explicit or implicit references to race or racial segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: AN ACT providing for an alternate method of financing the operation of a county law library in counties containing a city of the third class.
classification.summary: This act allows counties with a city of the third class to adopt an alternate method for financing the operation and maintenance of their county law library. This method is initiated by a petition from three-fourths of the licensed and practicing attorneys resident in the county, addressed to the circuit judge, recognizing the need for an adequate law library.

classification.title: An Act relating to the financing of county law libraries.
classification.summary: This act outlines an optional method for financing the operation and maintenance of county law libraries in Kentucky. It details the process for adopting this method, including a petition signed by licensed attorneys and a resolution from the fiscal court. Upon adoption, a county law library fee will be taxed as costs in various court actions (excluding examining and felony trials) and paid to the library trustees. The funds are designated as the county law library fund and are under the control of the trustees.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes a mechanism for funding county law libraries through fees added to court costs. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race, nor does it establish separate facilities or differential treatment for different racial groups. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1956.

executive sessions , and he shall execute all the orders of the

habits of any species , class , type or breed of wild life, either

partment may , by regulations :

CHAPTER 214
(Н. В. 227)
AN ACT relating to the " friend of the court" .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 403.090 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
(1) The fiscal court of any county may , by resolution ,
authorize the appointment of a "friend of the court " . If the
circuit court of the county has but one judge , the appoint-
ment shall be made by the judge . If the court has two or
more judges , the appointment shall be made by joint action
of the judges , at the general term . The appointment shall be
for a term of two years , subject to removal at the pleasure of
the appointing authority . Vacancies shall be filled by appoint-
ment of the appointing authority for the unexpired term . The
person appointed shall be a licensed practicing attorney . He
shall take the constitutional oath of office and shall give bond
in such sum as may be fixed by the appointing judge or judges .
(2) It shall be the duty of the friend of the court to super-
vise and enforce the payment of sums ordered or adjudged by
the circuit court in divorce actions to be paid for the care and
maintenance of minor children . All persons who have been

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the "friend of the court"

This appointee, a licensed practicing attorney, will be responsible for supervising and enforcing child support payments ordered by the circuit court in divorce actions. The appointment term is two years, with provisions for removal and filling vacancies. The act also appropriates funds for mosquito control.

This statute focuses on civil procedure and family law and does not contain any explicit or implicit racial components. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: CHAPTER 214
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties and powers of a "friend of the court" in divorce actions involving minor children. It details how payments for child care and maintenance should be handled, including potential redirection through the juvenile court. The friend of the court is responsible for investigating delinquent payments, ensuring proper care and maintenance of children, and reporting to the court. In divorce cases with minor children, the friend of the court may be requested to investigate circumstances affecting the children's rights and interests and provide recommendations to the judge. The statute also grants the friend of the court authority to secure court orders, rules, or citations.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and procedural aspects of a "friend of the court" role in divorce cases concerning child support and custody. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. The statute focuses on the legal framework for child welfare within the context of divorce proceedings. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

sary for the proper enforcement of orders and judgments in

the county , and shall have authority to cross-examine the
witnesses ; in the case of depositions taken on interrogatories ,

of the time and place of the taking of depositions in all divorce

(5) The friend of the court shall not directly or indirectly
represent any party to a divorce action except as he is herein
authorized to represent the minor children of parties to a
divorce action , but if an allowance is made for the support
of a mother and her infant child or children , he may proceed
to enforce the payment of the allowance made to the mother
also .
(6) The fiscal court of any county which has authorized
the appointment of a friend of the court under this section
shall , by resolution , fix a reasonable compensation for the
friend of the court , and make a reasonable allowance for his
necessary supplies, equipment and expenses , payable out of
the general fund of the county , upon approval of the judge
or judges appointing him. The compensation of the friend
of the court in counties containing a city of the first class
shall not exceed $3,600 per annum , and the allowance for
expenses shall not exceed $ 1,200 per annum ; and in all other
counties the compensation shall not exceed $2,400 per annum ,
and the allowance for expenses shall not exceed $900 per
annum .

Section 3. Any urban renewal project undertaken pur-
suant to the preceding section shall be undertaken in accord-
ance with an urban renewal plan for the area of the project .

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 99.490 regarding urban renewal agencies
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 99.490 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes. It allows a community or housing commission to exercise the powers, functions, and duties of an agency for urban renewal projects if the council deems it efficient and in the public interest. The community or housing commission can delegate these powers, but not the power to issue bonds or acquire real property, which remains with the community. Bonds issued for these purposes can be revenue bonds. The statute also declares an emergency and takes effect upon passage and approval.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses urban renewal planning and the delegation of powers to housing commissions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is on the administrative and financial aspects of urban renewal projects. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it amends an existing section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS).

classification.title: AN ACT relating to insurance and amending KRS 304.099 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
classification.summary: This act amends KRS 304.099 concerning insurance policies. It requires policies covering subjects of insurance located or to be performed in Kentucky, placed by or through a licensed nonresident agent, to be countersigned by a duly licensed resident agent. It also stipulates that the countersigning resident agent and the nonresident agent shall share equally in the commissions derived from such policies, with exceptions for certain policies and bonds enumerated in KRS 304.101.

CHAPTER 218
(Н. В. 471)
AN ACT concerning regulation of the manufacture , sale , transportation ,
distribution and use of economic poisons and devices, including
fungicides , rodenticides and herbicides .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. As used in this Act, unless the context requires
otherwise :
( 1 ) "Economic poison " means any substance or mixture of
substances intended for preventing , destroying , repelling , or miti-
gating any insects , rodents , fungi , bacteria , weeds , or other forms
of plant or animal life or viruses ( except viruses on or in living
man or other animals ) which the director shall declare to
be a pest ;
(2) "Device " means any instrument or contrivance in-
tended for trapping , destroying , repelling , or mitigating insects
or rodents or destroying , repelling , or mitigating fungi , bacteria ,
or weeds , or such other pests as may be designated by the di-
rector , but not including simple , mechanical devices such as
rat traps or equipment used for the application of economic poi-
sons when sold separately therefrom ;
(3 ) " Insecticide " means any substance or mixture of sub-
stances intended for preventing , destroying , repelling or miti-
gating any insects which may be present in any environment
whatsoever ;
(4 ) "Fungicide" means any substance or mixture of sub-
stances intended for preventing , destroying , repelling or mitigat-
ing any fungi , or plant disease ;
( 5 ) "Rodenticide " means any substance or mixture of sub-
stances intended for preventing , destroying , repelling , or mitigat-
ing rodents or any other vertebrate animal which the direc-
tor shall declare to be a pest ;
( 6 ) "Herbicide" means any substance or mixture of sub

classification.title: CHAPTER 218
classification.summary: This statute outlines the registration requirements for economic poisons in Kentucky. It details the information that must be filed by registrants, including the name and address of the registrant and the manufacturer, the name of the economic poison, a copy of the labeling and claims, and potentially test results. It also specifies conditions for registration, such as allowing products with the same formula and claims to be registered as a single economic poison, and permitting changes to labeling or formulas under certain circumstances without re-registration. Registration expires annually on December 31st unless renewed.

building, boat , vessel or place supposed to contain economic

package or parcel of economic poison , or any device ;
(b ) To publish from time to time , in such forms as he

on official samples of economic poisons sold within the state ;
( c ) After due public hearing, to make , promulgate , alter ,
change , rescind such rules and regulations relating to the sale
and distribution of economic poisons as he may find necessary

of Agriculture , and any other state or agency thereof for the pur-

CHAPTER 220 441
WHEREAS , the damages claimed are in excess of the jurisdictional
amount limitations imposed by law on the Board of Claims ;
NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That Helen Klinglesmith , residing at 6009 Linda
Road in Louisville, Kentucky is authorized to proceed before
the Board of Claims against the Commonwealth of Kentucky
or the Department of Highways , or either of them for damages
arising out of an accident which occurred on the 4th day of
November , 1954, involving an agent or employe of the Depart-
ment of Highways ; that this action shall be commenced within
one year from the effective date of this Resolution and no plea
of limitations shall be interposed if commenced within that time ;
Section 2. That the claim shall be for a sum not in excess
of $ 5,000 ;
Section 3. That the Attorney General of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky may investigate this claim and settle it
before or after suit is filed if he deems it advisable ;
Section 4. That any amount recovered in this action ,
either by settlement or judgment , shall be paid out of the Road
Fund in the State Treasury upon warrant drawn by the Com-
missioner of Finance upon the State Treasurer ;
Section 5. That in the event the proceeding is had before
the Board of Claims and results in a final award either party
may appeal to the Franklin circuit court and from that court
to the Court of Appeals in the manner prescribed by law.
Approved February 28 , 1956

CHAPTER 222 443
WHEREAS , in May of 1953 , the American Hardware and Furniture
Mart , Bowling Green , Kentucky , furnished certain materials in the
amount of $74.80 to the Kentucky Department of Highways ; and
WHEREAS , through inadvertence , this account has never been paid ;
NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That the Department of Highways is authorized
and directed to pay to the American Hardware and Furniture
Mart of Bowling Green , Kentucky , out of the State Road Fund ,
the sum of $74.80 for materials furnished .
Section 2. Whereas , this claim is long overdue and to
cause further delay in payment would be an injustice and a bur-
den to a claimant , an emergency is hereby declared to exist and
this Resolution shall become effective when passed and approved
by the Governor .
Approved February 28, 1956
CHAPTER 222
(S. R. 19 )
A JOINT RESOLUTION authorizing and directing payment of a claim
of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company .
WHEREAS , in December , 1950 the Louisville and Nashville Railroad
Company , of Louisville, Kentucky , furnished certain services in the
amount of $270.05 to the Kentucky Department of Highways ; and
WHEREAS , through inadvertence , this account has never been paid ;
NOW, THEREFORE ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That the Department of Highways is authorized
and directed to pay to the Louisville and Nashville Railroad
Company , of Louisville , Kentucky , out of the Road Fund of the
department , the sum of $270.05 for services rendered .
Section 2. Whereas , this claim is long overdue and to
cause a further delay would be an injustice and a burden , an
emergency is hereby declared to exist and this Resolution shall
become effective when passed and approved by the Governor .
Approved February 28 , 1956

444 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 223
(S. R. 20 )
A JOINT RESOLUTION authorizing Mrs. Emma McCoun and Mrs.
Orene Swango Davis to sue the Commonwealth of Kentucky and
the Department of Highways , or either .
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That Mrs. Emma McCoun and Mrs. Orene
Swango Davis are authorized to sue , either jointly or severally ,
the Commonwealth of Kentucky and the Department of High-
ways , or either , in the Wolfe circuit court , for damages re-
sulting to each of them from personal injuries and medical
expenses caused by alleged negligence of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky and the Department of Highways in the construc-
tion and maintenance of Kentucky Highway No. 15 in Wolfe
county , Kentucky , on or about March 27, 1952 .
Section 2. Said suit , or suits, shall be for a sum or sums
not to exceed fifteen thousand ( $ 15,000.00 ) dollars in each case .
Section 3. The Attorney General may investigate the claim
or claims herein and settle each claim , or claims , before or after
suit is filed, if he deems it advisable . Any amount recovered in
said suit , or suits , or by settlement with the Attorney General ,
shall be paid out of the General Expenditure Fund in the State
Treasury upon warrant drawn by the Commissioner of Finance
upon the State Treasurer .
Section 4. In the event suit or suits are filed and judgment
rendered by the circuit court , either party , or parties , may appeal
to the Court of Appeals of Kentucky .
Section 5. No statement in this Resolution shall be con-
sidered by either the court or jury as an admission of negligence
upon the part of the Commonwealth , and this Resolution shall
not be introduced as evidence , nor commented upon by counsel
in argument to the jury .
Section 6. No plea of limitations shall be interposed .
Approved February 28 , 1956

Any recovered amounts will be paid from the General Expenditure Fund. Appeals are permitted, and the resolution itself cannot be used as evidence of negligence. No plea of limitations will be allowed.

classification.summary: This resolution authorizes Dorothy Burke, as administratrix of Harry Burke's estate, to sue the Commonwealth of Kentucky or the Department of Highways for damages resulting from Harry Burke's death. The death allegedly occurred due to the Department of Highways' negligence in maintaining a state-maintained bridge over Abbott's creek in Floyd county. The suit is limited to a sum not exceeding ten thousand dollars. The Attorney General is authorized to investigate and settle the claim. Any recovered amount will be paid from the General Fund. Either party may appeal a circuit court judgment to the Court of Appeals. The resolution explicitly states that it shall not be considered an admission of negligence by the Commonwealth or Department of Highways and shall not be introduced as evidence.

450 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
twenty - one years of age , Kelly Brooks McCuiston , William L.
Hargrove , Mrs. William L. Hargrove and Tom Hurt , jointly or
individually , are hereby authorized and permitted to sue the
Department of Highways and the Commonwealth of Kentucky ,
or either of them , in such sums as will fairly and reasonably com-
pensate each of them, but the total of all suits not to exceed the
sum of six thousand and eighty nine dollars , for such injuries and
damages as they may have sustained as a direct and proximate
result of the negligence , if any , of the Department of Highways
and the Commonwealth of Kentucky through the negligence of
their employes .
Section 2. That the action herein authorized and permitted
may be brought in the Calloway circuit court , same being the
circuit court having jurisdiction in Calloway county , Kentucky,
the county in which the damages and injuries were incurred . Said
suit shall be commenced within one year from the effective date
of this Resolution and no plea of limitations shall be interposed
if commenced within that time .
Section 3. That such cross -claims or counter -claims be per-
mitted as are allowable by the law and by and between the
parties involved , the Department of Highways and the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky .
Section 4. That any party to such action may appeal from
any judgment which may be entered by the Calloway circuit
court to the Court of Appeals of Kentucky as in any other civil
suit and this suit may be settled and adjusted by and with the
consent and approval of the Attorney General of Kentucky in the
same way as any civil action may be settled or adjusted . If
recovery is had by suit or compromise , the amount so adjudged
or compromised shall be paid out of the State Road Fund , upon
warrant drawn by the Commissioner of Finance upon the State
Treasurer .
Section 5. No statement in this Resolution shall be con-
sidered by either the court or jury as an admission of negligence
upon the Department of Highways or the Commonwealth of
Kentucky , and this Resolution or any portion thereof shall not be
introduced in evidence , nor commented upon by counsel in argu-
ment to the jury .
Approved February 28 , 1956

Section 1. That Mrs. Glenn Roy Nantz, of London,
Laurel county , Kentucky is hereby authorized to sue the
Commonwealth of Kentucky , in the circuit court held in
and for Laurel county , Kentucky , this being the county of
her residence, and the county in which the injuries and
damages complained of occurred , for damages to her person
caused by the negligence on the part of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky , acting through their agents , servants and em-
ployees , who were then acting within the scope of their
authority .
Section 2. That said suit shall be for a sum not exceed-
ing fifteen thousand dollars .
Section 3. That the Attorney General may investigate
the claim herein referred to and settle said claim before or
after suit is filed, if in his opinion such settlement is for
the best interest of the Commonwealth of Kentucky . Any
amount recovered in said suit or by said settlement with the
Attorney General shall be paid out of the General Fund
upon warrant drawn by the Commissioner of Finance upon
the State Treasury . In the event suit is filed and judgment
rendered by the circuit court , either party may appeal to
the Court of Appeals of Kentucky .
Section 4. No statement in this Resolution shall be con-
sidered by either the court or the jury as an admission of
negligence upon the part of the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky , and this Resolution shall not be introduced as evi-
dence , nor commented upon by counsel in the argument of
the case to the jury.
Approved February 28 , 1956

Section 1. Incorporated municipalities are authorized to
provide for, construct , and finance improvements , as herein de-
fined , according to the plan set forth in this Act . The authority
hereby conferred is not intended to be in derogation of any au-
thority otherwise conferred upon such cities , but is alternative
and in addition thereto . If a city has taken any step or steps under
any other law to provide for, construct or finance street im-
provements it may abandon the procedure under the other law,
if it so desires , and proceed under the provisions of this Act, if
it can do so without impairing any contractual right or con-
tractual rights .
Section 2. The term "governing body," as used in this Act ,
means and includes the legislative body of any city whether the
same be designated by applicable statutes as a general council ,
a common council , a city council , a board of trustees , a board of
commissioners , or otherwise .
The term " ordinance ” means and includes any ordinance
enacted in accordance with the general laws applicable to
ordinances of the class of city in question , and the form of
government thereof , and in accordance with the provisions of
this Act .
The terms " improvement ," and "project ," mean and in-
clude the new construction of streets , sidewalks , boulevards ,
avenues , roads , lanes, alleys, parkways , courts or terraces , and
curbs or gutters or storm or surface water sewers or drains
incident thereto , or the substantial reconstruction or widening
thereof , or sanitary sewers in or adjacent thereto .
The term "costs " as applied to any project undertaken
under this Act includes the cost of labor, materials , and
equipment necessary to complete the project in a satisfactory
manner , cost of land acquired , and every expense connected with
the project , including preliminary and other surveys , inspections
of the work, engineers' fees and costs , attorneys' fees , pre-
paration of plans and specifications , publication of ordinances
and notices , interest which will accrue on the bonds until
the due date of the first annual improvement assessment levied
in connection therewith , a sum equal to any discount in the sale
of the bonds ( if discount bids are authorized and permitted
by the governing body) , a reasonable allowance for unforseen

classification.title: AN ACT relating to school districts.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 160.045 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the transfer of property between school districts. It outlines the process for such transfers initiated by petitions from registered voters or property owners, or by the school boards themselves. If the boards cannot agree within ninety days, the Superintendent of Public Instruction will make the decision, considering factors like the wealth ratio of the territory, and the impact on educational programs.

CHAPTER 242
(H. R. 37)
A JOINT RESOLUTION authorizing civil action against the Common-
wealth in the interest of H. B. Wilson .
WHEREAS , it appears by affidavit that on April 11 , 1951 , H. B. Wilson
suffered permanent injury in an accident connected with road work
on the Harrisonville -Mt. Eden Road project Number 106-446 -ICA in
Shelby county ;
WHEREAS , it appears that the damages arising from the accident may
have been sustained as a proximate result of negligence on the part
of the Commonwealth or its employes ; and
WHEREAS , the amount of the damages claimed exceeds the jurisdiction
of the Board of Claims ;
NOW, THEREFORE ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That H. B. Wilson, is authorized to sue the
Commonwealth of Kentucky and the Department of Highways ,
or either , in the Shelby circuit court , for damages allegedly
resulting from negligence on the part of employes of the De-
partment of Highways in connection with roadwork in Shelby
county on April 11, 1951 ; that said action will be commenced
within one year from the effective date of this Resolution and
that no plea of limitations will be interposed if commenced
within that time .
Section 2. That suit shall be for a sum not in excess of
fifteen thousand dollars .

CHAPTER 243
(S. B. 124)
AN ACT to prevent unfair competition and unfair trade practices in the
sale of cigarettes; to prohibit sales of cigarettes below cost with
intent to injure competitors or to destroy or substantially lessen
competition ; to protect and stabilize the collection of taxes on the
sale of cigarettes and of revenues from the licensing of persons en-
gaged in the sale of cigarettes ; to confer powers and impose duties
on the state Department of Revenue and on persons , as herein de-
fined , engaged in the sale of cigarettes at wholesale or retail ; and to
provide remedies and impose penalties for violations thereof .
WHEREAS , unfair , dishonest , deceptive, destructive and fraudulent
business practices existing in transactions involving the sale of ,
offer to sell , or inducement to sell , cigarettes in the wholesale and
retail trades in this state have been and are demoralizing and dis-
organizing the said trades ; and
WHEREAS , the advertising , offering for sale , or sale of cigarettes below
cost in the wholesale and retail trade with the intent of injuring
competitors or destroying or substantially lessening competition , is
an unfair and deceptive business practice ; and
WHEREAS , such practices adversely affect the collection of taxes from
the sale of cigarettes and license fees imposed on wholesalers and
retailers and others engaged in the sale of cigarettes; and

484 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
WHEREAS , it is hereby declared to be the policy of this Commonwealth
to promote the public welfare by prohibiting such sales , and to
foster and encourage competition by prohibiting unfair , dishonest ,
deceptive, destructive , fraudulent discriminatory practices by which
fair and honest competition is destroyed or prohibited , and it is the
purpose of this Act to carry out that policy in the public and in the
Commonwealth's interest ; therefore ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. SHORT TITLE . This Act shall be known and
may be cited as the "Act to Prevent Unfair Cigarette Sales In
Kentucky."
Section 2. DEFINITIONS . When used in any part of this
Act , the following words , terms and phrases and any variations
thereof required by the context , shall have the meaning ascribed
to them in this section except where the context clearly indicates
a different meaning :
( a ) "Person " shall mean and include any individual , firm ,
association, company , partnership , corporation , joint stock
company , club , agency , syndicate , the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky and any municipal corporation or other political subdivision
of this state , trust , receiver , trustee , fiduciary , conservator .
( b ) “ Commissioner " shall mean the Commissioner of
Revenue of the Commonwealth of Kentucky .
( c ) " Department ” shall mean the Department of Revenue .
( d ) "Cigarettes ” shall mean and include any roll for smok-
ing made wholly or in part of tobacco , irrespective of size or
shape and whether or not such tobacco is flavored , adulterated
or mixed with any other ingredient , the wrapper or cover of
which is made of paper or any other substance or material , ex-
cepting tobacco .
( e ) "Wholesaler ” shall mean any person , wherever resident
or located , who sells cigarettes at wholesale or distributes cig-
arettes to be sold at retail , and it includes any manufacturer ,
distributor , jobber , broker , agent or other person , whether or not
enumerated in this subsection, who so sells or so distributes
cigarettes .
( f ) "Retailer ” shall mean and include any person who sells
cigarettes in this state to a consumer or to any person for any
purpose other than resale .

nes ; ( b ) where cigarettes are advertised , offered for sale , or sold

in such cigarettes and said advertising , offer to sell , or sale shall
state the reason thereof and the quantity of such cigarettes
advertised , offered for sale, or to be sold; (c) where cigarettes
are advertised ; offered for sale , or sold as imperfect or damaged ,
and said advertising , offer to sell , or sale shall state the reason
therefor and the quantity of such cigarettes advertised , offered
for sale, or to be sold ; (d) where cigarettes are sold upon the

acting under the order or direction of any court .
Section 7. TRANSACTIONS PERMITTED TO MEET
LAWFUL COMPETITION .
( a) Any wholesaler may advertise , offer to sell or sell cigar-
ettes at a price made in good faith to meet the price of a com-
petitor who is rendering the same type of service and is selling
the same article at cost to the said competing wholesaler as de-
fined by this Act . Any retailer may advertise , offer to sell or sell
cigarettes at a price made in good faith to meet the price of a
competitor who is selling the same article at cost to the said com-
peting retailer as defined in this Act. The price of cigarettes adver-
tised , offered for sale, or sold under the exceptions specified in
section 6 shall not be considered the price of a competitor and
shall not be used as a basis for establishing prices below cost,
nor shall the price established at a bankrupt sale be considered
the price of a competitor within the purview of this section .
(b ) In the absence of proof of the actual cost to the said
competing wholesaler or to the said competing retailer , as the case
may be , such cost may be presumed to be the lowest cost to
wholesaler or the lowest cost to retailer , as the case may be ,
within the same trading area as determined by a cost survey made
pursuant to section 9 ( b ) .
Section 8. SALES CONTRACTS VOID . Any contract ,
expressed or implied , made by any person in violation of any of
the provisions of this Act, is illegal and void and no recovery
shall be had thereon .
Section 9. ADMISSABLE EVIDENCE .

490 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
assess in favor of the plaintiff and against the defendant the cost
of suit including reasonable attorney's fees . In such action it
shall not be necessary that actual damages to the plaintiff be
alleged or proved , but where alleged and proved , the plaintiff in
said action , in addition to such injunctive relief and costs of suit,
including reasonable attorney's fees , shall be entitled to recover

(b ) In the event that no injunctive relief is sought or re-
quired , any person injured by a violation of this Act may maintain
an action for damages and costs of suit in any court of general
jurisdiction .
Section 12. STATE DEPARTMENT OF REVENUE ;
POWERS AND DUTIES . The state Department of Revenue
shall prescribe , adopt , and enforce rules and regulations for the
enforcement of this Act and it is empowered to and may from
time to time undertake and make or cause to be made one or more
cost surveys for the state or such trading area or areas as it shall
define and when each such cost survey shall have been made by
or approved by the said department it shall be permissible to use
such cost survey as provided in section 7 ( b ) and 9 ( b ) of this
Act .
The department may , upon notice and after hearing , revoke
or suspend any license issued under provisions of KRS 137.040-
137.050-137.070 and the rules and regulations of said depart-
ment promulgated thereunder , for failure of any person to com-
ply with any provisions of this Act or any rule or regulation
adopted thereunder .
All of the powers vested in the Commissioner and Depart-
ment of Revenue by the provisions of the Cigarette Tax Law
shall be available for the enforcement of this Act To Prevent
Unfair Cigarette Sales In Kentucky .
Any person aggrieved by any decision , order or finding of
said department , suspending or revoking any such license , may
appeal therefrom to the Kentucky Tax Commission by filing a
petition of appeal with said commission in the manner and form
and within the time and subject to such terms and conditions as
said commission shall by rules prescribe .

CHAPTER 244
(Н. В. 86)
AN ACT relating to the publication , sale and distribution of comic books .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. It is hereby declared to be the policy of the
legislature to restrain the publication ; sale and distribution to
minors of comic books devoted to crime , terror , physical tor-
ture , brutality , or illicit sex . It is found that such comic books
and publications are a contributing factor to juvenile crime
and a basic factor in impairing the ethical and moral develop-
ment of our youth .
Section 2. Any person who shall publish or distribute for
sale any book , pamphlet or magazine consisting of narrative
material in pictorial form , colored or uncolored , and commonly
known as comic books , the content of which is devoted to or
principally made up of pictures or accounts of methods of crime ,
terror , physical torture , brutality or illicit sex , shall , upon con-
viction , be fined not more than one thousand dollars or im-
prisoned not more than one year , or both .
Section 3. ( a ) Any person who shall knowingly sell , lend ,

classification.summary: This act prohibits the sale, lending, giving away, showing, advertising for sale, or commercial distribution of comic books, as defined in Section 2, to persons under the age of eighteen. It establishes penalties for violations, including fines and imprisonment. The act also creates a presumption of intent to violate the law if such comic books are displayed where minors can see them, or if a person possesses six or more identical copies. Furthermore, it allows for legal action to enjoin the publication, distribution, or sale of prohibited articles, with provisions for seizure and destruction of such materials.

CHAPTER 245 493
CHAPTER 245
(Н. В. 121)
AN ACT relating to walking horses being exhibited in the state of
Kentucky .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. The term "walking horse" as used in this
section shall be construed to include only that breed of horses
known as walking horses .
The term "handler " as used in this section shall be construed
to mean that person or persons who are in charge of grooming ,
preparing and readying said horse for competition .
Section 2. No walking horse shall be permitted to compete
or exhibit in any exhibition or fair either for profit or pleasure ,
if said horse's front legs or hoofs shows evidence of burns , drugs ,
lacerations , any sharp pointed instrument , or any pain in-
flicting device .
Section 3. It shall be the duty of the assigned ringmaster
in charge of any such exhibition or competition to properly
inspect the front legs and hoofs of each entry in each class or
event. Said inspection shall be for the purpose of determining
whether there is any evidence of burns , drugs , lacerations , any
sharp pointed instrument , or any pain inflicting device ap-
pearing on said animal .
Section 4. If any such evidence appears to the satis-
faction of the ringmaster , he shall immediately bar said horse
from competition , and notify the sheriff of said county of said
violation . The handler of said horse shall be fined not less
than ten dollars nor more than one hundred dollars or imprisoned
for ten days or both . For the second and each subsequent
offense he shall be imprisoned for thirty days .
Section 5. Any ringmaster who fails to perform these
duties , and permits the commission of any of the offenses stated
in section 2, shall be fined not less than ten dollars nor
more than one hundred dollars for each offense allowed .
Section 6. This section shall in no wise repeal any laws

CHAPTER 246
(Н. В. 136)
AN ACT relating to city officials and employes .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 89.490 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
The mayor and each commissioner shall execute a bond
upon which an action may be maintained by any person inter-
ested in the keeping of the covenants therein contained . The
bonds shall be approved by the county judge and filed as mat-
ters of public record . In cities of the third class the bonds shall
be in the penal sum of ten thousand dollars , and shall run to
the city. In cities of the second and fourth classes the bonds
shall be in a penal sum fixed by the county judge , and shall run
to the Commonwealth . The board of commissioners shall re-
quire bonds with adequate surety, in such amounts as it may
deem advisable , from any city employe whose duties , in the
opinion of the board , require a bond for faithful performance .
Premiums on bonds may , in the discretion of the board of com-
missioners , be paid by the city .
Section 2. Section 89.510 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :
The mayor shall preside at meetings of the board of com-
missioners and perform such other duties consistent with his
office as may be imposed by the board . He shall be recognized
as the official head of the city for all purposes including the
serving of civil process . In time of public danger or emergency
he may , with the consent of the board of commissioners , take
command of the police , maintain order and enforce the laws .
Section 3. Section 89.560 of the Kentucky Revised Stat-
utes is amended to read as follows :

classification.title: Amendment to Kentucky Revised Statutes Section 95.430 and 95.440 concerning Police and Fire Departments
classification.summary: This statute amends sections of the Kentucky Revised Statutes related to the organization and qualifications of police and fire departments in cities of the second and third classes. It outlines the control of these departments by the city legislative body, the establishment of rules, the appointment of personnel, and the setting of salaries. It also specifies qualifications for applicants, including being a qualified voter, ability to read, write, and understand English, sobriety, integrity, and being an orderly, law-abiding citizen. For cities of the second class, there's an age limit for appointment.

While it mentions requirements like literacy in English and being a law-abiding citizen, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race or any discriminatory provisions based on race. The qualifications listed are general and do not appear to be designed to disenfranchise or segregate any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 247
(Н. В. 341 )
AN ACT relating to trees , plants , weeds and pests .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 249.190 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows :
If any person who holds land on which Canada or Nodding
thistles are growing and likely to ripen seed , neglects or refuses to
cut and destroy them , any person who considers himself ag-
grieved or about to be injured by the neglect or refusal ,
shall give five days' notice in writing to the person who holds
the land, to cut and destroy the Canada or Nodding thistles .

classification.title: AN ACT relating to trees, plants, weeds and pests.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 249.190 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the destruction of Canada or Nodding thistles. It requires landowners to cut and destroy these weeds if they are growing and likely to ripen seed. If a landowner neglects this duty, any person who feels aggrieved or is about to be injured by this neglect can provide five days' written notice to the landowner to cut and destroy the thistles.
